text: We had a problem. We made a proposal. We negotiated. We signed. The savings are four million.
To begin with, the problem. Moving on, the proposal. That said, the negotiation was tough. As a result, we signed. To sum up: four million in savings.

Thank you all for the time. I want to walk you through the supplier-consolidation programme end to end, so let me signpost where we are going. To begin with, the context. Twelve months ago we were running eleven overlapping regional suppliers, and the crux of the problem was duplication: overlapping contracts, inconsistent lead times, and no single point of accountability. Moving on to the proposal. We built a fully costed three-year consolidation plan, and the overarching aim was simple: fewer partners, higher volume, better rates. Now, the negotiation. This is where it became interesting. We held the rate provided that the supplier committed to the volume, and we made the penalty clause non-negotiable. That said, we were reasonable: we offered to halve the penalty for a faster lead time, as our main bargaining chip. As a result, both sides signed in good faith. So, to bring the threads together, the throughline of the whole programme is this: every decision, from the first proposal to the final amendment, was underpinned by real figures. To sum up, we have moved from eleven suppliers to four, the projected savings stand at four point two million over three years, and the contract is signed. In essence, that is the full story, from standpoint to signature. I am happy to take questions on any stage.

Hi Fabiana, it is Marcus. I have just come out of the leadership review, and I wanted to give you the upshot straight away: they loved it. What landed, more than anything, was the coherence. You took them from the original problem all the way to the signed contract without a single gap, and the signposting made it effortless to follow. A couple of reflections. First, the way you used discourse markers to frame each stage, to begin with, moving on, that said, consequently, gave the whole story a clear throughline; nobody got lost. Second, the recap at the end was sharp. Summarizing the four point two million in savings and the move from eleven suppliers to four, in two sentences, was exactly the kind of synthesis the board responds to. One small piece of advice for next time: when you segue from the negotiation to the results, leave a half-second pause; it lets a big number land. By and large, though, this was a complete, end-to-end story, well told. Genuinely, this is the strongest you have presented all year. Congratulations on closing the programme, and let us debrief properly on Thursday. Talk soon.

Fabiana opens by signposting the whole story end to end. To begin with, she gives the context: eleven overlapping suppliers and the crux of the problem. Moving on, she presents the proposal: a fully costed three-year consolidation plan. Then she covers the negotiation, holding the rate and trading a bargaining chip for a faster lead time. As a result, both sides signed. To sum up, she recaps the headline figures and ties the whole throughline together, from standpoint to signature.
